text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Next-Generation Sequencing — Solid Tumor Panel

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PATIENT INFORMATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Patient Name:         Margaret Okonkwo
Date of Birth:        [on file]
Sex:                  Female
Ordering Physician:   Genitourinary Oncology Service
Diagnosis (submitted): Renal Cell Carcinoma, Clear Cell Type — Stage IV (cT3bN2M1)
                       Right Kidney
Specimen Source:      CT-Guided Core Needle Biopsy, Right Renal Mass
Specimen ID:          [assigned at accessioning]
Specimen Type:        Formalin-Fixed Paraffin-Embedded (FFPE) Tissue Cores
Tumor Cellularity (estimated): 55% (adequate for analysis)
Platform:             Comprehensive Genomic Profiling (CGP), Hybrid-Capture Based
                      324-Gene Panel (DNA and RNA)
Sequencing Coverage:  Mean 500x (tumor); 150x (matched blood / germline)
Analysis:             Somatic variant detection, copy number alterations (CNA),
                      structural variants / fusions, tumor mutational burden (TMB),
                      microsatellite instability (MSI), genomic loss of heterozygosity
                      (gLOH), PD-L1 IHC (22C3 pharmDx, concurrent)
Germline Confirmation: Matched normal (peripheral blood) analyzed in parallel

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SOMATIC ALTERATIONS DETECTED:

  1. VHL  p.R161*  (c.481C>T)                              PATHOGENIC — TIER I
     Variant type:   Nonsense (stop-gain)
     Allele frequency: 48%
     Exon:           3
     ClinVar/CGC:    Known pathogenic driver mutation in clear cell RCC
     Interpretation: Loss-of-function mutation in the von Hippel–Lindau tumor
                     suppressor gene. VHL inactivation is the canonical initiating
                     event in the majority (>80%) of clear cell RCCs. Loss of pVHL
                     protein abrogates ubiquitin-mediated degradation of HIF-1α and
                     HIF-2α, resulting in constitutive upregulation of downstream
                     hypoxia-response gene products including VEGF, PDGF, and GLUT1.
                     Combined with chromosome 3p loss (see CNA below), this mutation
                     represents complete biallelic inactivation of VHL. This finding
                     is consistent with the clinical and morphologic diagnosis of ccRCC
                     and supports VEGFR-targeted therapy sensitivity. Allele frequency
                     of 48% is consistent with near-clonal presence within the tumor.

  2. PBRM1  c.2977+1G>T                                    PATHOGENIC — TIER I
     Variant type:   Splice site (canonical donor site, intron 23)
     Allele frequency: 42%
     Predicted effect: Exon 23 skipping / intron retention leading to premature
                     termination (loss-of-function)
     Interpretation: PBRM1 encodes BAF180, the chromatin-targeting subunit of the
                     PBAF SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex. PBRM1 is the second
                     most commonly mutated gene in ccRCC after VHL (mutated in
                     ~40% of cases). Loss of PBRM1 impairs transcriptional regulation
                     and DNA damage response, promoting tumor progression. Prior
                     immunohistochemistry on this specimen demonstrated loss of PBRM1
                     nuclear expression, consistent with this somatic mutation.
                     Emerging data suggest PBRM1-deficient ccRCC may have altered
                     sensitivity to immune checkpoint blockade. Retained BAP1 expression
                     by IHC (see pathology report) indicates a PBRM1-mutant / BAP1-
                     wild-type molecular subtype, historically associated with a
                     relatively more favorable prognosis within advanced ccRCC compared
                     to the BAP1-mutant subtype, though this patient has high-grade
                     sarcomatoid features.

  3. SETD2  p.L1916Rfs*15  (c.5745delC)                    PATHOGENIC — TIER II
     Variant type:   Frameshift deletion (1-bp deletion)
     Allele frequency: 31%
     Interpretation: SETD2 encodes a histone H3 lysine 36 trimethyltransferase
                     (H3K36me3), functioning as a tumor suppressor and key component
                     of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling axis. SETD2 mutations occur
                     in approximately 10–15% of ccRCCs and are enriched in higher-grade
                     tumors. Loss of SETD2 function is associated with impaired
                     mismatch repair recruitment, microsatellite instability in a
                     subset of cases (not detected here), and altered epigenetic
                     regulation of oncogene expression. The subclonal allele frequency
                     (31%, lower than VHL at 48%) suggests this may be a secondary
                     evolutionary event acquired after VHL loss. SETD2 loss, together
                     with PBRM1 loss, defines a common epigenetically dysregulated
                     molecular subtype of ccRCC. No validated targeted therapy is
                     directly indicated by this finding beyond existing standard-of-care
                     options.

  4. KDM5C  p.T724M  (c.2171C>T)                           VUS — TIER III
     Variant type:   Missense
     Allele frequency: 18%
     Interpretation: KDM5C (also known as JARID1C) encodes a histone H3 lysine 4
                     demethylase located on chromosome Xp11.22. Recurrent somatic
                     mutations in KDM5C have been identified in ccRCC, although most
                     are classified as variants of uncertain significance pending
                     functional validation. The relatively low allele frequency (18%)
                     suggests a subclonal or possibly minor clone event. KDM5C loss
                     has been implicated in epigenetic dysregulation of gene expression
                     programs. No direct therapeutic target or clinical actionability
                     at this time. Monitored for reclassification as functional data
                     accumulate.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS DETECTED:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  1. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion (High-Level Copy Number Loss)      PATHOGENIC
     Chromosomal region: 9p21.3
     Interpretation: CDKN2A encodes both p16INK4a (a CDK4/6 inhibitor promoting
                     G1/S cell cycle arrest) and p14ARF (a positive regulator of
                     p53 via MDM2 inhibition). Homozygous deletion results in complete
                     loss of both tumor suppressor functions. CDKN2A deletions are
                     found in approximately 15–20% of ccRCCs and are associated with
                     aggressive biology, higher Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP grade, and sarcomatoid
                     differentiation — the latter of which is prominently present in
                     this patient's tumor (22% sarcomatoid component confirmed on
                     nephrectomy specimen). This CNA is consistent with the high-grade
                     and sarcomatoid morphology observed histologically. CDK4/6
                     inhibitors (e.g., Ibrance, Kisqali) are not currently
                     standard-of-care in RCC and no clinical trials specifically
                     enrolling CDKN2A-deleted RCC are noted as immediately accessible;
                     however, this finding may inform future clinical trial eligibility.

  2. Chromosome 3p — Loss (Whole-Arm Loss of Heterozygosity)         PATHOGENIC
     Relevant genes at locus: VHL (3p25.3), PBRM1 (3p21.31), SETD2 (3p21.31),
                               BAP1 (3p21.31)
     Interpretation: Chromosome 3p loss is the hallmark chromosomal event in ccRCC,
                     occurring in >90% of cases. In conjunction with somatic VHL
                     p.R161* (above), this copy number loss establishes complete
                     biallelic VHL inactivation (mutation + LOH). The 3p deletion
                     also encompasses the PBRM1 and SETD2 loci, providing the second
                     hit for these tumor suppressors. This finding is entirely expected
                     and pathognomonic for ccRCC. The genomic LOH score (gLOH 18%;
                     see below) partially reflects this large-scale allelic imbalance.

  3. Chromosome 5q — Gain (Low-Level Amplification)                  LIKELY PATHOGENIC
     Interpretation: Chromosome 5q gain is one of the most common recurrent copy
                     number gains in ccRCC, occurring in approximately 60–70% of
                     cases. The relevant genes in this region include SQSTM1, NSD1,
                     and several genes involved in mTORC1 signaling regulation.
                     Chromosome 5q gain has been associated with better response to
                     VEGFR-targeted therapies in some retrospective analyses, though
                     prospective validation is lacking. No targeted therapy is directly
                     indicated by this finding.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
STRUCTURAL VARIANTS AND FUSIONS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  No pathogenic or likely pathogenic structural variants or gene fusions detected.
  Specifically:
    - No MET amplification detected (MET copy number: 2 [diploid])
    - No FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3, or FGFR4 fusions or activating rearrangements
    - No ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3 fusions
    - No TMPRSS2 or other RCC-relevant fusion transcripts identified

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ADDITIONAL NEGATIVE FINDINGS (SELECTED GENES OF CLINICAL RELEVANCE):
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  KRAS:    No mutation detected (wild-type)
  NRAS:    No mutation detected (wild-type)
  BRAF:    No mutation detected (wild-type)
  BAP1:    No pathogenic somatic mutation detected; retained expression by IHC
             (confirming intact BAP1 function in this specimen)
  MTOR:    No activating mutation detected
  TSC1:    No pathogenic mutation detected
  TSC2:    No pathogenic mutation detected
  PIK3CA:  No activating mutation detected
  PTEN:    No loss detected
  TP53:    No pathogenic somatic mutation detected
  MET:     No mutation; no amplification (see above)
  HIF2A (EPAS1): No activating mutation detected

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) STATUS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Result:          MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Method:          CGP-based MSI analysis (109 intronic homopolymer loci)
  Mismatch Repair: Proficient MMR (pMMR)
  IHC correlation: MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 — all retained nuclear expression
                   (confirmed on pathology specimen; see separate pathology report)
  Interpretation:  MSS/pMMR status indicates the tumor does not harbor DNA
                   mismatch repair deficiency. This finding has two main implications:
                   (1) Keytruda monotherapy based solely on MSI-H/dMMR
                   indication (FDA-approved pan-tumor indication) is not applicable;
                   (2) the robust response rate with PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade
                   (Yervoy + Opdivo) in this patient is anticipated to be
                   driven by the high PD-L1 expression, sarcomatoid features, and
                   PBRM1 loss rather than MMR deficiency.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB):
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  TMB:             6.2 mutations/megabase (mut/Mb)
  Classification:  INTERMEDIATE-LOW
  Reference:       TMB-High threshold (FDA approved biomarker): ≥10 mut/Mb
  Interpretation:  The TMB of 6.2 mut/Mb does not meet the threshold (≥10 mut/Mb)
                   for the pan-tumor Keytruda TMB-H indication. This is
                   consistent with the broader ccRCC landscape, which is typically
                   characterized by low-to-intermediate TMB. Importantly, ccRCC
                   immune sensitivity appears to be largely independent of TMB and
                   is instead driven by tumor microenvironment immune infiltration,
                   PD-L1 expression, and specific genomic co-alterations (e.g.,
                   PBRM1 loss, sarcomatoid differentiation). The TMB result does not
                   change the therapeutic strategy for this patient.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GENOMIC LOSS OF HETEROZYGOSITY (gLOH):
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  gLOH Score:      18% (intermediate)
  Interpretation:  gLOH of 18% is in the intermediate range. A gLOH score ≥16%
                   has been used as a PARP inhibitor sensitivity biomarker in
                   ovarian and breast cancers (HRD proxy); however, gLOH is not
                   a validated biomarker for renal cell carcinoma. The genomic LOH
                   in this case largely reflects the chromosome 3p arm-level loss
                   characteristic of ccRCC rather than a homologous recombination
                   deficiency signature. PARP inhibitor therapy is not indicated
                   based on this finding in the ccRCC context.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PD-L1 IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (CONCURRENT, 22C3 PHARMADX):
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Tumor Proportion Score (TPS):  40%
  Combined Positive Score (CPS): Estimated 55 (HIGH)
  Interpretation:  High PD-L1 expression (CPS 55) is consistent with the enriched
                   immune infiltration characteristic of sarcomatoid ccRCC. PD-L1
                   overexpression, in conjunction with sarcomatoid differentiation
                   and PBRM1 loss, supports clinical benefit from ipi +
                   nivo immunotherapy per CheckMate 214 sarcomatoid subgroup
                   data. This result was determined concurrently on the core biopsy
                   specimen and is consistent with the prior biopsy IHC result
                   (previously reported CPS ~55).

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GERMLINE ANALYSIS (MATCHED NORMAL, PERIPHERAL BLOOD):
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Germline testing was performed on a matched peripheral blood specimen using
  a focused hereditary cancer gene panel.

  Results — No pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants detected in:
    VHL       — No pathogenic germline variant (rules out hereditary VHL disease)
    SDHA      — No pathogenic germline variant
    SDHB      — No pathogenic germline variant
    SDHC      — No pathogenic germline variant
    SDHD      — No pathogenic germline variant
    FH        — No pathogenic germline variant (rules out hereditary leiomyomatosis
                 and renal cell cancer / HLRCC syndrome)
    TSC1      — No pathogenic germline variant
    TSC2      — No pathogenic germline variant
    MET       — No pathogenic germline variant (rules out hereditary papillary RCC)
    FLCN      — No pathogenic germline variant (rules out Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome)
    BRCA1     — No pathogenic germline variant
    BRCA2     — No pathogenic germline variant
    BAP1      — No pathogenic germline variant (germline BAP1 tumor predisposition
                 syndrome excluded)
    PBRM1     — No pathogenic germline variant detected (somatic only)

  Interpretation:  No hereditary cancer predisposition syndrome identified. The
                   VHL, PBRM1, and SETD2 mutations detected in this analysis are
                   somatic (tumor-only) in origin. Genetic counseling referral is
                   not required based on these findings. The patient's West African
                   ancestry and remote smoking history were noted; however, hereditary
                   ccRCC syndromes are not implicated by the germline analysis.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SUMMARY OF ALTERATIONS AND TIER CLASSIFICATION:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  Gene       Alteration                Variant Type         AF     Tier
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  VHL        p.R161* (c.481C>T)        Nonsense            48%    I
  PBRM1      c.2977+1G>T               Splice site         42%    I
  SETD2      p.L1916Rfs*15             Frameshift          31%    II
  KDM5C      p.T724M                   Missense (VUS)      18%    III
  CDKN2A     Homozygous deletion       Copy number loss     —     I
  Chr 3p     Arm-level LOH             Copy number loss     —     I (supportive)
  Chr 5q     Low-level gain            Copy number gain     —     II
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  No actionable drug-matched alterations beyond standard VEGFR-targeted
  therapy, mTOR inhibition, and immune checkpoint blockade.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  STANDARD-OF-CARE RELEVANCE:
  • VHL loss + 3p LOH: Confirms ccRCC diagnosis; supports VEGFR-targeted
    therapy sensitivity (suni, Votrient, cabozantinib, axitinib) and
    HIF-2α inhibitor (belzutifan) eligibility in future lines.
  • PBRM1 loss: Retrospective data (McDermott et al., NEJM 2018; Braun et al.,
    Nature Medicine 2019) suggest PBRM1-loss may be associated with differential
    response to Sutent vs. Tecentriq in combination regimens; PBRM1 loss
    has also been correlated with immune-inflamed tumor microenvironment features
    consistent with checkpoint inhibitor benefit.
  • High PD-L1 (CPS 55) + sarcomatoid differentiation: Strong rationale for
    first-line Yervoy + nivo (CheckMate 214 sarcomatoid subgroup
    demonstrated significant ORR and OS benefit).
  • CDKN2A homozygous deletion: Associated with aggressive biology and sarcomatoid
    features; does not alter standard first-line treatment selection at this time.

  CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATIONS (INFORMATIONAL):
  • HIF-2α inhibitor (belzutifan): FDA-approved for VHL disease-associated RCC;
    emerging efficacy data in sporadic ccRCC with VHL loss (LITESPARK program).
    VHL p.R161* mutation makes this patient a potential candidate for belzutifan
    in later treatment lines if available.
  • PBRM1-stratified trials: Several ongoing trials use PBRM1 status for
    stratification or enrichment in immunotherapy combination studies.
  • CDKN2A deletion: May confer eligibility for CDK4/6 inhibitor trials in RCC
    (investigational).

  NO ACTIONABLE GERMLINE FINDINGS REQUIRING ONCOLOGIC GENETIC COUNSELING.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QUALITY METRICS:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Tumor nucleic acid yield:      Adequate (>500 ng DNA input)
  Tumor cellularity:             55% (above minimum threshold of 20%)
  Mean sequencing coverage:      500x (tumor), 150x (germline)
  Uniformity (% bases >100x):    97.3%
  Sequencing quality score:      Pass
  Analysis QC:                   All QC metrics within acceptable ranges

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
METHODS NOTE:
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  This report was generated using hybrid-capture next-generation sequencing of
  a 324-gene panel targeting clinically relevant cancer genes, including full
  exonic coverage of key tumor suppressors and oncogenes and select intronic
  regions for fusion/rearrangement detection. Somatic variants are reported at
  ≥5% allele frequency with ≥50x depth at the variant locus. Copy number
  alterations and structural variants are identified using bioinformatic
  algorithms applied to on-target and off-target read depth. MSI and TMB
  are calculated from sequencing data using validated computational methods.
  PD-L1 IHC was performed concurrently on FFPE sections using the 22C3
  pharmDx assay (Dako/Agilent), FDA-approved for PD-L1 CPS scoring in
  multiple tumor types. Germline analysis was performed on matched peripheral
  blood DNA with a focused hereditary cancer panel.

  This report has been reviewed and approved by a board-certified molecular
  pathologist and molecular genetic pathologist. Results should be interpreted
  in the clinical context by a qualified oncologist.
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
END OF REPORT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
